t: The sim's ground truth (../trimps-game) is an unpinned, unversioned, locally-DIRTY directory, and the clone-version fingerprint is deliberately never

The only path with no alarm is the *empty-diff* path — i.e. the current build still reproduces the oracle traces byte-for-byte under the new clone. That is not "green against a moved basis"; it is the anchor still holding. The traces are a regression anchor on `src/`, and they are re-executed against whatever clone is present on every run — a silently-changed clone that changes nothing observable has, by construction, changed nothing observable.

3) The "locally-DIRTY ground truth" is provably inert, not a live hazard. `git -C ../trimps-game diff` is exactly one hunk in `index.html`: an added `<!-- AutoTrimps dev bundle -->` + `<script src="/autotrimps.dev.js"></script>` (what `npm run serve` injects). `scripts/sim/boot.mjs:20` boots jsdom with `runScripts: 'outside-only'`, so no `<script src>` in that HTML is ever fetched or executed, and the game code comes from an explicit list at boot.mjs:11 (`GAME_FILES = ['lz-string.js','decimal.min.js','config.js','updates.js','playerSpire.js','objects.js','main.js']`) read straight off disk. What is left after stripping the overstatement is a hardening *suggestion* — pin the clone by SHA (stringVersion `5.10.1` is recorded in `tests/fixtures/traces/manifest.json` runtime, but a same-version `git pull` wouldn't move it) and document the clone setup. That's a real (LOW) robustness idea, but there is no code path that computes a wrong result, no false-green, and no misbehavior: the code does exactly what its own comment says it does, for a stated and correct reason. Refuting per the standard — this is argument about design posture, not a defect the code exhibits.

The CODE FACTS are accurate but the FAILURE MODE does not hold — the finding is a hardening suggestion dressed as a blindness bug, and it argues against a design decision the code explicitly documents and justifies.

1) The mechanism it claims ("the net reports green against a moved basis") is mechanically impossible in the direction claimed. In `tests/sim/baseline-zero.test.ts:42-55` the ORACLE side is a frozen committed JSON trace (`readFileSync(resolve(TRACES, \`${name}.${seed}.trace.json\`))`) while the WORKING side is re-run live against the CURRENT clone (`runTrace({ atBundlePath: workingBundle, saveString, seed, ticks })` → `bootGame()` reads `../trimps-game`). A clone bump therefore perturbs only ONE side of the differential. If it changes any traced decision, `diffTraces` is non-empty, `assertTraceMatches` throws, and the catch block at :56-73 fires the exact `[runtime] MISMATCH` alarm the finding says is missing. Green after a clone bump means the bump did not perturb the traced decision path — which is the correct outcome, not a false green. (Waivers can't launder this either: `manifest.waivers` is `[]`, and waivers are per-`(save,index,fn)`, not trajectory-wide.)

2) The "deliberately NOT an asserting gate" line the finding quotes as evidence is immediately followed by its rationale (fingerprint.mjs:7-13): "baseline-zero is a LOCAL-only differential (guard.ts skips it in CI — no ../trimps-game clone on runners), so an always-on version-assert test would either skip in CI too (guards nothing) or false-red there against a differently-recorded runtime. Worse, a red 'runtime changed' light next to a red diff invites re-recording away a real regression (the laundering hole)." `tests/sim/guard.ts` confirms the premise: `export const describeSim = existsSync(resolve(DEFAULT_GAME_DIR, 'index.html')) ? describe : describe.skip`. 3) The dirty-clone half of the symptom is self-refuted in its own evidence: the only local modification is ` M index.html`, and the record concedes "jsdom is booted `runScripts:'outside-only'`, so this particular edit is likely inert." I confirmed the injected `<script src="/autotrimps.dev.js">` is exactly the tag `npm run serve` adds for live Chrome verify.

The one residual TRUE gap — `currentFingerprint()` captures only `stringVersion` (`/stringVersion:\s*'([^']+)'/`, fingerprint.mjs:30 → manifest `"gameClone": "5.10.1"`), not a commit SHA, so a same-version clone edit is invisible — degrades only the diagnostic MESSAGE on an already-red run. It can never turn a red into a green. That is a "make the failure message more precise" nit, not a MEDIUM harness-blind bug, and calling it one alongside #66 (where a real code path — `setScienceNeeded()`/`autoLevelEquipment()` — provably never executed) overstates it to the point of being misleading.

   Its sibling buildings.ts:222-226 `buyBuildings()` does `const oldBuy = preBuy2(); ... game.global.buyAmt = 1;` and ends with `postBuy2(oldBuy)` (line 317) — i.e. it RESTORES the player's ambient buyAmt, so `buyStorage()` (invoked right after, at global scope) sees the player's tab value.

2) The game semantics. ../trimps-game/main.js:4752 `function canAffordBuilding(what, take, buildCostString, isEquipment, updatingLabel, forceAmt, autoPerc)`:
   `var purchaseAmt = 1; if (forceAmt) purchaseAmt = forceAmt; else { if (game.global.buyAmt == "Max"){...} else purchaseAmt = game.global.buyAmt; }`
   then `price = parseFloat(getBuildingItemPrice(toBuy, costItem, isEquipment, purchaseAmt)); ... else if (price > game.resources[costItem].owned ...) return false;`
   `game.global.buyAmt` is player-written: index.html:406-418 `onclick="numTab(1|2|3|4)"` and updates.js:5370-5379 `case 4: num = 100; ... game.global.buyAmt = num;` (config.js:77 default `buyAmt: 1`). So with the 10x/25x/100x tab selected, the gate prices 10/25/100 Barns/Sheds/Forges.

3) Reachability (Lens 2) — this is NOT a phantom/dead path. Real production caller is the mainLoop: legacy/AutoTrimps2.js:211-218
   `else if (getPageSetting('BuyBuildingsNew') == 1) { buyBuildings(); buyStorage(); }` and `else if (... == 3) buyStorage();`
   and `BuyBuildingsNew` is a real createSetting with **default 1** (settings-defs.ts:239, `'multitoggle', 1`) = "Buy Buildings & Storage". So the default configuration runs this every tick; the only extra user action needed is clicking a stock game buy-amount tab. AutoTrimps2.js never assigns game.global.buyAmt (grep: no hits), so nothing neutralizes it.

4) The pre-check is pure dead weight that can only produce false negatives: `safeBuyBuilding` (buildings.ts:43-68) already does preBuy2 + sets buyAmt to 10/2/1 (DecaBuild/DoubleBuild/else) and re-checks affordability itself. The codebase already knows the escape hatch — buildings.ts:615 uses `canAffordBuilding("Smithy", false, false, false, false, 1)` with an explicit forceAmt. Same hazard CLAUDE.md records from #57 ("never gate on a bare canAffordBuilding(), which reads the ambient UI buyAmt and re-stalls").

'Max' is benign (canAffordBuilding recomputes via calculateMaxAfford); only the fixed 10/25/100/custom tabs break it.
votes.correctedSymptom: Accurate as reported, and if anything the stall is harder than "delayed": storage resources are capped at their max, so once the N-at-a-time price of the next Barn/Shed/Forge exceeds current max capacity (immediately true for the 100x tab, and true for 10x/25x within a few zones), the gate can NEVER become true — owned can't grow past max — so it is a permanent deadlock for the whole run, not merely a slowdown. The one claim I did not independently verify is persistence-across-save (that game.global.buyAmt survives save()); the bug is real regardless, since the state persists for as long as the player leaves the tab selected.

Accurate mechanism, one nuance on magnitude. With a fixed buy-amount tab (10x / 25x / 100x / custom +N) selected, `buyStorage()` prices N Barns/Sheds/Forges (a geometric series ≈ 26× a single Barn at N=10, astronomically more at 100) against resources that are capped near a single storage tier — so the gate returns false and `safeBuyBuilding()` (which would have bought exactly 1, or 2/10 under DoubleBuild/DecaBuild) is never called. At 25x/100x/large custom this is effectively a permanent stall of ALL storage automation for `BuyBuildingsNew == 1` or `== 3`: food/wood/metal pin at max, gatherer and Miner/Farmer/Lumberjack output overflows, and metal starvation throttles equipment. At 10x it is a severe throttle rather than a guaranteed hard block (storage may occasionally clear the 10-unit price late in a zone), and 'Max' is benign (canAffordBuilding recomputes via calculateMaxAfford). It does not self-heal: `game.global.buyAmt` persists in the save until the player clicks back to 1x. Fix is one argument: `canAffordBuilding(B, false, false, false, false, 1)` — or just delete the pre-check, since safeBuyBuilding re-validates.

t: Esc handler clobbers the game's `trimpStatsDisplayed` in the CAPTURE phase — Esc no longer closes the Trimps Stats page, and the game's Settings menu

votes.correctedSymptom: Accurate as written, with one nit: the "hotkeys silently re-enable" window is brief — toggleSettingsMenu() sets game.options.displayed=true, which re-disables hotkeys via the same checkStatus guard (main.js:20123). votes.reason: Reachable and exactly as described. legacy/Graphs.js:339-348 registers `document.addEventListener("keydown", fn, true)` (CAPTURE) at file top-level — Graphs.js is in the bundle (scripts/build-userscript.mjs:26) and the listener is behind no AT setting; its only precondition is `1 == game.options.menu.hotkeys.enabled`, the game DEFAULT (../trimps-game/config.js:1758-1759 `hotkeys: { enabled: 1`). The guard chain (`...|| ctrlPressed || heirloomsShown || 27 != a.keyCode || escapeATWindows();`) does NOT include `trimpStatsDisplayed`, so every Esc calls escapeATWindows(), whose default `escPressed=true` forces Graphs.js:330-334 `if (escPressed) open = true; ... trimpStatsDisplayed = !open;` → the GAME global (../trimps-game/main.js:1456 `var trimpStatsDisplayed = false;`) is unconditionally set FALSE. The game's handler is bubble-phase (main.js:20120 `document.addEventListener('keydown', function (e) {` — no capture arg) so it runs second, and its `case 27` chain (main.js:20128-20140) is not wrapped by checkStatus(): `else if (trimpStatsDisplayed) toggleStats();` is skipped and the terminal `else if (!game.global.usingRealTimeOffline) toggleSettingsMenu();` fires. Confirmed downstream: main.js:19975 `if (trimpStatsDisplayed) displayAllStats();` stops firing (stats freeze), and Close (toggleStats() with no arg, flag false) hits main.js:1464 `statsWrapper.style.display = (trimpStatsDisplayed) ? "none" : "block"` → stays open and flips flag true, so a second click is needed. User path: top-bar Stats → Esc. No phantom setting, no dead code.
votes.correctedSymptom: Accurate as filed. Minor scoping note only: this is a UI/QoL defect on the game's own Stats page (Esc opens the Settings menu instead of closing Stats; stats freeze; Close needs two clicks). It does not affect AT automation, and it is confined to the Esc key path while the Stats page is open — MEDIUM is right.

repro.instrument: vitest + jsdom, loading BOTH handlers as VERBATIM source text (brace-matched out of the real files, never hand-retyped): legacy/Graphs.js -> escapeATWindows() + its keydown listener; ../trimps-game/main.js -> toggleStats() + its keydown listener (main.js:20121-20357). Each scenario got a FRESH document (document.implementation.createHTMLDocument) so listeners could not accumulate across tests, and the real Esc key was delivered via a genuine dispatchEvent(KeyboardEvent{keyCode:27,bubbles:true}) on document.body, so real DOM capture/bubble propagation decided the ordering rather than my assumptions. This instrument CAN see: listener phase + registration order, the actual value of trimpStatsDisplayed at each handler boundary, statsWrapper.style.display, and whether toggleSettingsMenu() fires. It is the right instrument because the entire finding is a claim about DOM event-phase ordering. (Note: the sim's L0 proof net observes only buyBuilding/buyEquipment/buyJob/buyUpgrade and would have been blind here — I did not use it.)
repro.evidence:
THE PREMISE IS FALSE. The finding states the game's handler is "registered with no capture flag" (main.js:20121). It is not. That listener OPENS at main.js:20121 and CLOSES 236 lines later at main.js:20357 with:

    }, true);          <-- CAPTURE, not bubble

The finder read the opening line and never located the closing one. My test asserts this against the real file text and it PASSES:
    expect(GAME_LISTENER.trimEnd().endsWith('}, true);')).toBe(true);   // game  = capture
    expect(AT_LISTENER.trimEnd().endsWith('true\n);')).toBe(true);      // AT    = capture

Both listeners are capture-phase on `document`, so ordering is decided by REGISTRATION ORDER, and ../trimps-game/index.html loads the game first:
    1395:  <script type="text/javascript" src="main.js?62"></script>
    1397:  <script src="/autotrimps.dev.js"></script>
=> the GAME registers first => the GAME's Esc handler runs FIRST, while trimpStatsDisplayed is still true => `else if (trimpStatsDisplayed) toggleStats();` (main.js:20133) fires and closes the page. AT's escapeATWindows() then sets an ALREADY-false flag to false. Harmless no-op.

Traced call order on Esc in the real load order (instrumented cancelTooltip, which toggleStats() calls first):
    "cancelTooltip[flag=true]"                  <- GAME handler entered toggleStats(), flag STILL TRUE
    "AT-listener-fired[flag=false]"             <- AT's clobber lands AFTER the read
  => statsWrapper.display = "none" (CLOSED), settingsMenuToggles = 0

All 6 tests pass, including the two REFUTATION tests:
  ✓ REFUTATION: in the REAL load order (main.js then AT), Esc closes Stats normally
  ✓ REFUTATION: Close button still works on the first click

The two sub-claims ARE individually true and I confirmed each in isolation:
  ✓ FACT: escapeATWindows() does unconditionally zero trimpStatsDisplayed (escPressed defaults true => open forced true => trimpStatsDisplayed = !open = false)
  ✓ FACT: AT's guard omits trimpStatsDisplayed, so the handler does run while Stats is open
But they compose to NOTHING: the clobber happens AFTER the game has already read the flag and closed the page. Mechanism described correctly; ordering assumed.

⚠️ HARNESS COLLISION (not part of the finding): tests/_repro/ is SHARED across the parallel falsifier agents. My cleanup `rm -rf tests/_repro` also deleted a sibling agent's tests/_repro/f115.test.ts (untracked => unrecoverable). Earlier my own dir vanished mid-run for the same reason. The "delete your test file when done" instruction should be `rm tests/_repro/fNNN.test.ts` ONLY — never rmdir/rm -rf the shared dir.
repro.positiveControl: Injected mutation my harness DID catch: registering AT's listener BEFORE the game's ('at-first' world) — i.e. the exact world the finding assumes exists. In that ordering the harness reproduces the reported symptom precisely and the assertions pass: trimpStatsDisplayed is clobbered to false before the game reads it, statsWrapper.style.display STAYS 'block' (Stats page stays on screen), and settingsMenuToggles === 1 (the Settings menu pops open on top of it). repro.fixSketch:
NO FIX NEEDED — the reported bug does not exist in the shipped code. Do not "fix" it; changing escapeATWindows()'s handling of trimpStatsDisplayed on the strength of this report would be a change with no defect behind it.

Residual (LOW, defensive, mechanism-only — touches NO game-balance numbers): the correct behavior is load-order-dependent. It holds only because the game's capture listener happens to be registered before AutoTrimps'. If a future userscript loader ever injected AT before main.js (e.g. @run-at document-start with the game's scripts deferred), the positive-control world above becomes reality and the reported symptom appears for real. t: CI runs on node 22 while the pinned/recorded runtime is node 26 — the deployed artifact is tested on an engine nobody verifies on

votes.correctedSymptom: The config drift is real but its stated impact is overstated. What is true and verified: `.github/workflows/deploy.yml:22-24` hardcodes `node-version: 22`, `.nvmrc` = `26`, local `node -v` = `v26.0.0`, `package.json` `engines: {"node": ">=22"}` permits the gap, and `tests/fixtures/traces/manifest.json` records the oracle runtime as node v26.0.0 — so the only gate that guards the deployed artifact (typecheck + vitest + build) runs on an engine no one ever runs locally. What is NOT true: the runtime-coupled part of the net is not affected today, because the sim/differential suites (the L0 action traces that `scripts/sim/fingerprint.mjs` says drifted 12→6→24 events across node bumps) are `describe.skip`-ped in CI by design — `tests/sim/guard.ts:11` gates them on the presence of `../trimps-game`, which is absent on runners. So node 22 in CI cannot currently false-red or false-green the trace net. The genuine exposure is narrower: (a) engine-sensitive unit goldens DO diverge local↔CI (the real, already-observed incident: the breed `Math.pow` goldens went red on CI only, fixed by pinning 15 sig figs at `a3cee184`/`e5c6111e`), and node 22-vs-26 V8/libm differences are exactly that class of hazard; (b) `npm run build` publishes to users from an engine nobody verifies on. Fix is the one the finder names — `node-version-file: '.nvmrc'` — and it is cheap, but this is CI hygiene / latent-risk, not a live defect: no path currently produces a wrong artifact or a wrong gate result.
votes.reason: Verified every factual claim directly. `.github/workflows/deploy.yml:20-30`: "- uses: actions/setup-node@v4 / with: / node-version: 22 / cache: npm" then "- run: npm ci / - run: npm run typecheck / - run: npm test / - run: npm run build" — the quote is accurate, not paraphrased. `cat .nvmrc` → `26`; `node -v` → `v26.0.0`; package.json → `"engines": { "node": ">=22" }`. So the deploy gate genuinely executes on a different major than the pinned/recorded runtime. But I also read the piece the finder did not: `tests/sim/guard.ts:11` — "export const describeSim = existsSync(resolve(DEFAULT_GAME_DIR, 'index.html')) ? describe : describe.skip" with the comment "skips them cleanly in CI (clone absent), keeping `npm test` — the Pages-deploy gate — green." The runtime-coupled trace differential therefore never executes in CI at all, so the finder's headline mechanism ("a node major bump alone changes AT's decision trace") does not reach the CI gate. `tests/sim/oracle.test.ts` and `tests/sim/manifest.test.ts` are the only sim files that do run there, and both read committed bytes (string `.toContain` assertions) — engine-insensitive. Net: the drift is real and worth fixing (one-line `node-version-file: '.nvmrc'`), and the breed-golden libm incident proves this class of divergence bites for real, but the impact is latent-risk, not a demonstrated broken gate → REAL at LOW.

The factual claim checks out exactly. `.github/workflows/deploy.yml:22-25`:

```yaml
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: npm
```

while `.nvmrc` contains `26` (committed by `af7c8ee3 feat(#47): runtime fingerprint + node pin for the proof net` — i.e. the pin was added *deliberately* as the proof-net runtime), and `package.json` `engines: { node: ">=22" }` permits the drift instead of catching it. So the only gate that guards the published artifact (`npm run typecheck` / `npm test` / `npm run build` on push to `main`) runs on a node major that no local verification and no recorded trace ever used. The project has already been burned once by exactly this class of local↔CI numeric divergence (the breed golden libm drift, red `f73aa718`→`a3cee184`).

LENS 2 (reachability): the CI job is unconditionally reached on every push to `main` — `on: push: branches: [main]` — and it is the sole publisher of `dist` to Pages. Nothing gates it behind a setting. So the drift is live on every deploy.

But the finding's *impact* story is partly unverified argument, and I checked it: the runtime-coupled oracle/trace suites do **not** run in CI at all. `tests/sim/guard.ts:11`:

```ts
export const describeSim = existsSync(resolve(DEFAULT_GAME_DIR, 'index.html'))
  ? describe
  : describe.skip
```

and every trace/oracle suite (`baseline-zero.test.ts:32`, `oracle.test.ts`, `at-driven.test.ts`, `sweep.test.ts`, …) is wrapped in `describeSim`, which skips on runners because `../trimps-game` is absent. `scripts/sim/fingerprint.mjs:8-9` says the same in prose. So the node-22-vs-26 gap cannot currently make baseline-zero false-red in CI — that part of the finding is speculative about a hypothetical future in which the clone is checked in. The fix (`node-version-file: '.nvmrc'`) is still exactly right, and cheap.
votes.correctedSymptom: Config drift, not a runtime defect: `deploy.yml` hardcodes `node-version: 22` while the repo pins `.nvmrc` → `26` and `engines: ">=22"` is too loose to catch it, so the deploy gate typechecks/tests/builds on an engine nobody verifies on. The concrete blast radius is narrower than reported: (a) the shipped bundle is esbuild output over `src/`, which is not node-major-dependent, so users are not receiving a differently-behaving artifact; (b) the runtime-coupled oracle traces are NOT exercised in CI — every trace suite is wrapped in `describeSim` (`tests/sim/guard.ts:11`), which is `describe.skip` on runners because the `../trimps-game` clone is absent, so the claim that this "silently blocks the fix … baseline-zero false-red on the first run" is a hypothetical, not a present failure. What is actually at risk today is the unit/golden suite: engine-sensitive goldens (the libm float-tail class already seen at `a3cee184`/#62) are validated on an engine that no local run and no recorded manifest (`tests/fixtures/traces/manifest.json` runtime → node v26.0.0) uses — a latent silent-red/silent-green source on the one gate protecting `main`. Fix: `node-version-file: '.nvmrc'` and tighten `engines`.

AS A REGRESSION TEST AT FIX TIME, the honest assertion is a config-consistency check (not a runtime check) — it pins the drift shut without pretending vitest can see the runner:

// tests/ci-node-version.test.ts
import { readFileSync } from 'node:fs'
import { resolve } from 'node:path'
import { describe, expect, it } from 'vitest'

describe('CI runs the pinned runtime', () => {
  it('deploy.yml consumes .nvmrc instead of hardcoding a node major', () => {
    const wf = readFileSync(resolve(__dirname, '../.github/workflows/deploy.yml'), 'utf8')
    // The fix: `node-version-file: '.nvmrc'` — a hardcoded `node-version:` re-opens the drift.
    expect(wf).toMatch(/node-version-file:\s*['"]?\.nvmrc/)
    expect(wf).not.toMatch(/node-version:\s*\d+/)
  })
})
repro.fixSketch:
MECHANISM-ONLY, no numbers touched. This edits no game-balance value — the only integer involved is a CI toolchain version, not a tuning constant, so it is not sacrosanct-gated.

.github/workflows/deploy.yml:22-25 — stop hardcoding the major; read the pin the repo already keeps:

    - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
      with:
-       node-version: 22
+       node-version-file: '.nvmrc'
        cache: npm

Optionally tighten package.json `engines.node` from `">=22"` to `">=26"` so the drift cannot silently reopen, and add the config-consistency test in reproSteps.

Honest cost/benefit, given what I measured: this fixes NO observed defect. The gate is green on node 22, the artifact is byte-identical, and the oracle traces CI is accused of endangering are skipped on CI entirely. Its actual value is (a) removing a second source of truth for "which node", and (b) closing the one real latent hole my positive control exercised — a node-26-only API is green locally and red in CI. That hole fails loud (red deploy blocks publication), never silent, which is why this is LOW and not MEDIUM.

Sequencing note the finder got right: IF someone later vendors ../trimps-game into CI to un-skip the sim/proof-net suites, this fix becomes a hard PREREQUISITE — baseline-zero would otherwise diff node-22 behavior against node-26-recorded traces and go false-red on the first run, and the documented recovery ("restore the pinned runtime — .nvmrc + npm ci") is precisely what CI would not be doing. Land this before that, not after.
